feedback.text: When consumers purchase digital contentsuch as movies, series, or video gamesthere is a reasonable expectation that these products will remain accessible over time, especially when terms like lifetime access, ownership, or digital library are used during the purchase process. In reality, however, this access is often revoked due to platform shutdowns, licensing expirations, or company acquisitions. Consumers lose access to content they believed they had permanently acquired, even though there is no technical or legal reason preventing continued use. This violates the fundamental understanding of ownership in a digital environment. This problem is particularly evident in the gaming sector. Even single-player games are frequently restricted by DRM or mandatory online authentication. When servers are shut down, the game becomes unusableeven if the technical infrastructure for local offline play exists and no online component is necessary. Multiplayer titles present additional challenges. In the past, players were often able to host their own local or private servers, enabling long-term access and community support even after official services were discontinued. Today, however, this is often deliberately restricted. Once central matchmaking or authentication servers are removed, entire products become inaccessible, despite the existence of dedicated communities willing and able to keep them alive. This loss of digital access and cultural content is unnecessary and deeply unfair. Another growing issue undermining digital fairness is the use of misleading freemium or premium media promotions. Many digital platforms advertise engaging article headlines or teasers to attract clicks. Users are then asked to accept or watch advertisementsonly to discover afterwards that the content is locked behind a paywall. This practice wastes time, creates frustration, and erodes trust in online services. It is a form of deceptive design that exploits consumer interest and misleads users about what they are actually being offered. These examples show that the current EU legal framework, while valuable, does not sufficiently protect consumers from unfair digital practices. Users are routinely misled, locked out of content, or manipulated by opaque licensing and access models. This erodes consumer rights in the digital age.
